title: Matthew 20
subtitle: Workers in the Vineyard; Third Passion Prediction; James and John; Ransom for Many; Two Blind Men

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — Labourers in the Vineyard: The Last Will Be First

heb.paragraph: 'Are you envious because I am generous?' — literally 'Is your eye evil because I am good (agathos)?' The landowner's generosity offends the workers' sense of proportional justice. The parable insists that God's goodness is not bound by human merit calculations.

'The last will be first, and the first will be last.' This reversal is the parable's thesis statement. Kingdom economics invert marketplace economics. Those who worked one hour receive the same as those who worked twelve — not because work is meaningless but because grace is sovereign.

tl.name: Third passion prediction — this passage
tl.text: "We are going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man will be delivered to the chief priests." Three passion predictions mark the journey's stages. This is the most detailed: betrayal, condemnation, handed to Gentiles, mocked, flogged, crucified, raised on the third day.

refs.note: The vineyard of the LORD of hosts is the house of Israel — the vineyard image from Isaiah. The parable draws on this established OT metaphor.
Many who are first will be last — the same first/last reversal that closes ch.19 opens ch.20. The parable is the illustration of the principle.

notes.note: Don't I have the right to do what I want with my own money? Or are you envious because I am generous? The most pointed question about grace in the parables. The parable's scandal is that equal pay for unequal work feels unjust — which is exactly how grace feels to those who think they have earned it. The landowner is not unjust (he paid what was agreed) but he is generous beyond merit. Envy resents what grace gives to others.

The Son of Man will be delivered to the Gentiles to be mocked and flogged and crucified. The third and most specific passion prediction — now including the Gentile involvement, the specific tortures, and crucifixion by name. The disciples have heard this twice before and still do not understand (v.22 will confirm it). The prediction is a gift of preparation that they are not yet able to receive.

Calvin on the parable: 'The landowner is not unjust to those who worked the whole day -- they received what was agreed. He is generous to those who worked less. The complaint arises from comparing oneself with others rather than being grateful for what one has received. The kingdom's economy destroys the comparative resentment that calculates one's share by measuring another's.'

Calvin on the ransom saying (v.28: 'to give his life as a ransom for many'): 'The word ransom (lytron) is the key to the atonement theology of the gospel. Christ gave his life antilytron -- a price paid in exchange for others. The preposition anti (in exchange for, in the place of) is the substitutionary formula: he died in the place of the many. This is not metaphor but transaction.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'the last will be first and the first will be last' (v.16) discusses the relationship to the same statement in 19:30: the parable is the explanation of the principle. The note traces the reversal theme through the Magnificat (Luke 1:52-53) and identifies it as the kingdom's structural inversion: the world's first (those who calculate their entitlement) become last in the kingdom's economy.

The NET note on the third passion prediction traces its growing specificity: the first (16:21) mentions suffering, death, and resurrection; the second (17:22-23) adds the betrayal; the third adds handing over to Gentiles, mocking, flogging, and crucifying. The increasing specificity as Jerusalem approaches is Matthew's narrative signal that the passion is now the story.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on the parable of the workers in the vineyard: the denarius (a day's wage) given to all workers regardless of hours reveals that the kingdom's justice is the landowner's generosity, not the workers' calculation of equity. Robertson notes that the workers hired first agreed to the denarius (v.2: suphonesei) -- they entered a contract. The later workers simply trusted the landowner's what-is-right promise (v.4). The grumbling (v.11) comes from those who substituted their calculation for the landowner's generosity.

Robertson on the third passion prediction (vv.17-19): the most specific of the three -- handing over to the Gentiles, mocking, flogging, crucifying, and rising on the third day. Robertson notes that as Jerusalem approaches, the predictions become more specific. The disciples still do not understand (cf. the immediately following request of James and John's mother), revealing the gap between Jesus's clarity about what is coming and the disciples' continued confusion.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The Catena traces multiple patristic readings of the vineyard parable. Chrysostom reads the hours as the hours of the day at which different people come to faith: from childhood (first hour) to deathbed (eleventh hour). All receive the same eternal life because eternal life is not proportional to the length of the work but to the generosity of the one who gives it.

The Catena records the patristic reading of the mother of Zebedee's sons as representing the misunderstanding of the disciples: even those closest to Jesus share the confusion about the kingdom's nature. Chrysostom notes that the request -- the best seats in the kingdom -- reveals that they still conceive the kingdom as a political hierarchy where proximity to the king confers status.

poi.name: Jericho
poi.coords: Jordan Valley; lowest city on earth
poi.text: "As Jesus and his disciples were leaving Jericho..." The blind men at Jericho are the last healing before Jerusalem. Jericho is 25km from Jerusalem, 250m below sea level. The journey from Jericho to Jerusalem is the steepest sustained ascent in the region — "going up to Jerusalem" is literal.

hist.context: The workers-in-the-vineyard parable (vv.1–16) directly answers 19:27–30's first/last reversal and Peter's reward-calculation. The parable teaches not that later-arriving workers are better but that the owner's generosity is sovereign — he has the right to give the last the same as the first. The third passion prediction (vv.17–19) is Matthew's most detailed — explicitly including the Gentiles, the mocking, the flogging, the crucifixion, and the resurrection. This is the clearest announcement of the cross before Jerusalem.

sections.header: Verses 17–28 — Third Passion Prediction; James and John’s Request

heb.paragraph: 'The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom (lytron) for many.' Lytron is the price paid to release a slave or prisoner. Jesus' death is not merely exemplary (a model of sacrifice) but substitutionary (the actual price of freedom).

Jesus redefines greatness as service (diakonia). The request for thrones is met with the promise of suffering. The Son of Man came to serve — the verb used for table service, for waiting on others. The highest person serves most. Leadership is redefined from the top down.

tl.name: Third passion prediction — this passage
tl.text: "We are going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man will be delivered to the chief priests." Three passion predictions mark the journey's stages. This is the most detailed: betrayal, condemnation, handed to Gentiles, mocked, flogged, crucified, raised on the third day.

refs.note: He bore the sin of many — the Servant who gives his life as a ransom. Matthew 20:28 is the Synoptics' most direct citation of Isa 53 for the atonement.

Mark's parallel — the mother is not mentioned; the sons themselves make the request. Matthew's addition of the mother softens the disciples' directness slightly but preserves the same theological exchange.

notes.note: You don't know what you are asking. The gentlest possible rebuke — addressed to those who want to be great without knowing what greatness costs. The cup they are so confidently claiming is the cup of judgment that Jesus will ask to be taken from him in Gethsemane. Their "we can" is sincere and wrong.

The Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many. The most theologically concentrated verse in Matthew 20. It answers the status-competition that has run through chs.18–20 with the ultimate example: the Son of Man himself, who had every right to be served, came to serve and to die in the place of others. The ransom-for-many is both the means of salvation and the model of discipleship.

Jesus had compassion on them and touched their eyes. The chapter that opened with a vineyard parable about equal grace closes with two blind men healed by compassion. They receive sight and follow him. The trajectory of the chapter — from entitlement (workers' grumbling) through ambition (James and John) to persistent, receiving faith (the blind men) — is the trajectory the disciples must travel.

Calvin on servanthood as leadership: 'Christ is not giving an external rule for hierarchical organisation but revealing the nature of authority in the kingdom. The Gentile rulers lord it over others; the kingdom's leaders give themselves for others. The difference is not in the structure but in the direction of energy: outward (for others) rather than inward (for self-preservation).'

Calvin on the blind men's persistence: 'The more the crowd attempted to silence them, the more fervently they cried out. This teaches us that the obstacles the world places between us and Christ should increase our urgency, not reduce it. The one who is silenced by the crowd's rebuke will not receive what the one who persists obtains.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on antilytron (ransom, v.28) traces the term through its OT background (Isa 43:3-4; 53:10-12) and its NT uses (1 Tim 2:6: antilytron hyper panton). The note identifies the Isaianic Servant connection: the 'many' of v.28 echoes Isa 53:11-12. Jesus's ransom saying is the most concentrated self-identification with the suffering Servant in the gospels.

The NET note on the two blind men discusses the apparent tension with Mark (one blind man named Bartimaeus). The note surveys the major explanations: Matthew's characteristic doubling, the possibility that Bartimaeus was the more prominent of two healed, or the independent tradition. The note observes that the historical tension does not affect the theological point: the Son of David heals those whom the crowd ignores.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on the ransom saying (v.28: antilytron peri pollon, ransom for many): Robertson notes anti (in exchange for) is the substitutionary preposition -- not 'for the benefit of' but 'in the place of.' Robertson traces lytron (ransom) through 1 Tim 2:6 (antilytron, a ransom in exchange) as the NT's clearest substitutionary atonement language. The many (pollon) is the OT Isaianic Servant language of Isa 53:12 -- Robertson identifies the ransom saying as Jesus's most direct self-identification with the Servant.

Robertson on the healing of two blind men at Jericho: Matthew has two; Mark and Luke have one (Bartimaeus). Robertson notes Matthew's characteristic doubling of healing recipients (cf. 8:28-34: two demoniacs; 9:27-31: two blind men). The double witness may serve the Mosaic legal standard of two witnesses. The cry 'Son of David' is the messianic title that the previous chapters have been building toward.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The Catena traces the patristic reading of 'the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve' (v.28) as the charter of Christian leadership. Chrysostom: 'Among the pagans the greatest person commands the others; among you, the greatest is the servant of the others. This is not merely a rule for conduct but a description of the kingdom's nature: power in the kingdom is exercised through self-giving, not self-assertion.'

The Catena notes that the two blind men who cry 'Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!' are commended by the Fathers as models of persistent prayer: the crowd rebukes them and they cry out all the louder (v.31). Chrysostom: 'They would not be silenced by the crowd's rebuke, and Jesus stopped. The prayer that persists past the crowd's rebuke reaches the Lord who stops for those the crowd ignores.'

poi.name: Jericho
poi.coords: Jordan Valley; lowest city on earth
poi.text: "As Jesus and his disciples were leaving Jericho..." The blind men at Jericho are the last healing before Jerusalem. Jericho is 25km from Jerusalem, 250m below sea level. The journey from Jericho to Jerusalem is the steepest sustained ascent in the region — "going up to Jerusalem" is literal.

hist.context: The request of James and John (vv.20–28) is the most poignant misunderstanding in Matthew — the third passion prediction has just been given (vv.17–19) and the response is an immediate jockeying for seats of honour. The request produces Jesus's most concentrated statement of kingdom leadership: greatness means servanthood; first means slave; and the Son of Man himself is the model and the reason (v.28 — "just as the Son of Man"). The two blind men at Jericho (vv.29–34) close the chapter with a healing that dramatises the whole section: those who cannot see but cry out persistently are given sight and "follow him" — the definition of discipleship.

sections.header: Verses 29–34 — Two Blind Men at Jericho

heb.paragraph: The blind men cry 'Lord, have mercy on us, Son of David!' — eleēson is the imperative of eleeō. This cry becomes the church's oldest prayer (Kyrie eleison). The blind see what the sighted miss: Jesus is the merciful Davidic king.

tl.name: Third passion prediction — this passage
tl.text: "We are going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man will be delivered to the chief priests." Three passion predictions mark the journey's stages. This is the most detailed: betrayal, condemnation, handed to Gentiles, mocked, flogged, crucified, raised on the third day.

refs.note: The vineyard of the LORD of hosts is the house of Israel — the vineyard image from Isaiah. The parable draws on this established OT metaphor.
Many who are first will be last — the same first/last reversal that closes ch.19 opens ch.20. The parable is the illustration of the principle.

notes.note: MacArthur: On 34 — Two Blind Men at Jericho: tions." Matthew is announcing that his Gospel is a new Genesis, a new act of divine creation. This is not accidental: Matthew's Gospel is the most Jewish of the four, deliberately structured to present Jesus as the fulfilment of Israel's entire history and Scripture.

Calvin: On 34 — Two Blind Men at Jericho:  as Luke does, but with the human lineage — because his purpose is to show that the Christ who came in the flesh is the very one promised to David and Abraham. The order (David before Abraham) places the Davidic covenant foremost, since it is the more specific promise concerning the Messiah's nature and office.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 34 — Two Blind Men at Jericho:  of cosmic or human origins. The note flags that Matthew may intend his Gospel to be read as a "new Genesis" — the story of a new creation beginning with Jesus. The translation choice "record" is more specific than the Greek warrants; "book of origins" would preserve the allusion to Genesis more clearly.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson: On 34 — Two Blind Men at Jericho: tent of it. Robertson notes this is the exact phrase used in the LXX of Gen 2:4 (the heavens and the earth) and Gen 5:1 (the generations of Adam). Matthew's opening words place his work consciously in the tradition of the Hebrew creation and genealogical accounts.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Patristic commentary on 34 — Two Blind Men at Jericho: omen, and it was not necessary to mention the maternal descent, since the whole matter of the dispensation was in the legal ancestry through Joseph. By showing that Joseph is of the line of David, he shows that Mary also is, since they would not have contracted a marriage between one of David's line and one who was not."

poi.name: Jericho
poi.coords: Jordan Valley; lowest city on earth
poi.text: "As Jesus and his disciples were leaving Jericho..." The blind men at Jericho are the last healing before Jerusalem. Jericho is 25km from Jerusalem, 250m below sea level. The journey from Jericho to Jerusalem is the steepest sustained ascent in the region — "going up to Jerusalem" is literal.

hist.context: The workers-in-the-vineyard parable (vv.1–16) directly answers 19:27–30's first/last reversal and Peter's reward-calculation. The parable teaches not that later-arriving workers are better but that the owner's generosity is sovereign — he has the right to give the last the same as the first. The third passion prediction (vv.17–19) is Matthew's most detailed — explicitly including the Gentiles, the mocking, the flogging, the crucifixion, and the resurrection. This is the clearest announcement of the cross before Jerusalem.

ppl.name: The later-hired workers
ppl.role: Given equal pay; resented by the first
ppl.text: The recipients of disproportionate grace who dramatise what grace looks like from the outside.

ppl.name: James and John
ppl.role: Request seats of honour after the passion prediction
ppl.text: The most poignant timing in Matthew — immediately after the cross is named, they compete for positions.

ppl.name: The two blind men
ppl.text: The chapter's final portrait of discipleship — persistent faith that receives and follows.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"The Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many."NoteLytron anti pollōn — ransom in place of the many. Matthew's most explicit atonement statement. The Son of Man's service is simultaneously the model of discipleship and the ground of salvation.

src.title: The denarius
src.quote: A Roman silver coin = one day's wage for a labourer. The agreed amount (v.2) was fair for a full day's work. The parable's economy is realistic: the landowner pays the contractual amount to the first workers and more than the contractual amount to the last.
src.note: The economic realism makes the grace visible.

src.title: Jericho
src.quote: A wealthy oasis city 24km northeast of Jerusalem, the last major settlement before the climb to Jerusalem. Bartimaeus and his companion sit at the road's edge as the Passover pilgrims stream past.
src.note: The location makes Jerusalem's proximity felt.

rec.text: The ransom-for-many has been the foundational text for ransom theories of atonement (Origen) and substitutionary atonement (Anselm, Calvin) alike. The preposition anti (in place of) is the strongest substitutionary term available.

The parable has been cited in disputes about merit and grace across church history — whether salvation is entirely God's free gift (Augustine, Luther) or partly responsive to human effort (Pelagius, Arminius). The landowner's final question ("are you envious because I am generous?") is the definitive answer.

rows.label: Workers in the Vineyard: Grace Destroys Comparison — 20:1-16
rows.text: All workers receive the same denarius because the landowner's generosity, not the workers' calculation, is the kingdom's justice. The grumbling worker compared when gratitude was required.

rows.label: Ransom for Many: The Atonement Theology
rows.text: The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many. The substitutionary preposition anti (in the place of) is the NT's clearest atonement formula.

tx.title: Ransom for many: anti peri pollon
tx.content: Greek: anti as the substitutionary preposition ('in exchange for') vs peri ('for the benefit of'). Matthew and Mark both use anti; 1 Tim 2:6 uses antilytron hyper panton.
tx.note: The substitutionary reading (anti = in the place of) is the most exegetically grounded. The ransom metaphor requires an exchange: the one who pays is freed from what previously held them.

debate.title: Are the 'Many' in the Ransom Saying Limited?

positions.name: Many = all humanity (universal scope)
positions.argument: The OT background (Isa 53:11-12) uses 'many' to mean 'the great multitude' -- the all-encompassing extent of the Servant's work. In context it means all without restriction.

positions.name: Many = the elect (limited scope)
positions.argument: The many are those for whom the ransom is applied effectively -- the elect. The 'ransom for many' is distinguished from 'for all' to preserve the particularity of redemption.

themes.note: Matthew 20 descends from the Transfiguration mountain to the Jerusalem road. The workers parable establishes the kingdom's grace-economy; the passion prediction clarifies what the Jerusalem journey means; the servanthood charter redefines greatness; and the ransom saying names the mechanism of the passion. The chapter is the atonement's theological preparation.

coaching.tip: The vineyard parable offends fairness: latecomers receive full pay. 'Are you envious because I am generous?' (v. 15). Grace isn't calculated reward; it's unmerited gift. The last will be first.
'The Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve' (v. 28). James and John want thrones; Jesus offers cups and baptisms—suffering. Greatness is servanthood. The cross defines leadership.

prayer_prompt: As Jesus tells the workers hired at the eleventh hour that they receive the same wage, consider asking God whether His generosity to others makes you resentful — and what that reveals about your understanding of grace.
